summary: Jeff Atwood shares practical tips for improving the Windows XP command prompt experience. He walks through essential configuration changes like increasing buffer size, enabling QuickEdit mode, and expanding window dimensions. The post also covers lesser-known keyboard shortcuts including command history navigation, tab completion, and drag-and-drop file paths. For power users, he points to 4nt as a more capable shell replacement descended from 4dos.
key_points: The 'Open Command Window Here' right-click menu is one of the first quality-of-life improvements to add to any fresh Windows install
QuickEdit mode transforms copying and pasting in cmd.exe from painful to intuitive with mouse-based selection
Tab completion with SHIFT+TAB for reverse iteration works across all commands, not just specific ones
Drag and drop from Explorer windows directly inserts quoted paths into the command prompt
The F7 key provides a visual popup of command history rather than requiring arrow key navigation
money_quotes: Windows XP isn't known for its powerful command line interface.
And hoary old cmd.exe does have a few tricks up its sleeve that you may not know about.
Now we've got some room to actually see stuff!
QuickEdit mode enables copying from the command prompt by intuitively dragging and right clicking with the mouse.
But if you prefer Comic Sans here, be my guest!
If you're really a hard-core cmd.exe junkie (or maybe a UNIX user), you may want to look into the 4nt command shell replacement.
key_insight: Even Windows' notoriously weak command prompt becomes significantly more usable with a few configuration tweaks and knowledge of its hidden keyboard shortcuts.
